description: Naksha hooks — detect project context, validate HTML output, suggest follow-up commands

hooks.prompt:
Scan the current session for naksha memory blocks and apply any pending updates.

## 1. Find and apply naksha-memory-update blocks

Search the conversation above for HTML comment blocks matching this pattern:
  <!-- naksha-memory-update
  { "type": "...", ... }
  -->

For each block found, parse the JSON payload and apply it to .naksha/project.json.
Locate the file by checking .naksha/project.json from the current directory, then walking up to 3 parent directories.

**project_json_patch** — set a value at a dot-notation path:
  - Read the current project.json
  - Traverse the path (e.g. "constraints.grid" → project_json["constraints"]["grid"]) and set the value
  - If any intermediate key in the path is absent, create it as an empty object before descending (e.g. if "constraints" is missing, set it to {} first)
  - For array or object values, replace the full value at that path (no deep merge)
  - Write the updated JSON back to the same file

**browser_finding_write** — prepend a browser capture to the browser_findings array:
  - Read project.json
  - Prepend the finding object to the browser_findings array (create the array if absent)
  - Cap the array at 20 entries — remove the oldest entries from the tail if needed
  - Write back

If project.json cannot be located, or if any read/write fails, append a timestamped error line to ~/.naksha/hook-errors.log:
  [<ISO timestamp>] [<resolved-project-path or "unknown">] <description of what failed and why>
(Include the project.json path you attempted, even if the file was not found, so errors are attributable across multiple projects.)

If no naksha-memory-update blocks appear in this session, skip this step entirely.

## 2. Follow-up suggestion

If this session involved design work (designing, reviewing, scoring, or browsing a UI), suggest one or two relevant naksha commands as a follow-up, in one sentence.

If the file being written is an HTML file, check: (1) the tag has a lang attribute, (2) semantic elements are used (
,
,
,
,
,
) instead of only
, (3) all tags have alt attributes, (4) interactive elements use
